From the moment someone lays eyes on you in person, they are processing your body language, what you're wearing, how you move, how close you stand to them, what type of eye contact you think, how you shake hands, how you move your body while you're talking, how you move your body while you're listening. All those little breaths. Ooh, how you move, how you move back. The human brain is incredibly indebted. It's processing. And in order to be charismatic, it's about having appropriate body language, knowing how to move your body when you're talking and while you're listening. From what you wear to the eye contact to how you shake hands, right? So number one, it's about effective tonality. And number two, it's about having appropriate body language. And remember, you don't have to learn these. You already know them. You've already used each of these 10 tonalities countless times in your life, but you use them by accident or when you really felt them. Or you told someone a secret. You lowered your voice and all of a sudden you created a scarcity of information by lowering your voice because you felt that naturally. And once you do this, once you start using these tonalities on purpose, you will be shocked at how easy influence becomes. Bottom line. All right. So we can start with the first one. It's going to be number one first. I'm just going to introduce. Okay. The first tonality is the tonality of certainty. Okay. So this look now here's what I'm going to do. I'm going to explain what the tonality is and I'm going to give you examples of good tonality and bad tonality. And I'm going to ask you which one sounds better to you. It'll be plainly obvious. Okay. And then again, we'll revisit it a month or so from now. We're going to have the media surround and we're going to interact. Okay. So no one is certain. Let me explain to you what certainty is. The tonality of certainty is what you're so sure about something that you get that point of course to your prospect. Now remember that at the highest level, sales is the transference of emotion. That's what you're doing. And the emotion that you're transferring is the emotion of certainty. The certainty about your product is awesome. The certainty about you, the sales person, the awesome that you want to deal with you, get your break, get your best of what you do. The certainty about the company that stands behind the product. Certainty, certainty, three tens, right? And we have three elements of certainty. And the tonality of certainty doesn't sound like this. Okay. Let me tell you, this is the best product I have right now. This thing is going to solve all your needs. It's going to remove all your pain and it is by fall the best deal in the marketplace. I repeat that. Jeff, this is the best product out there. It's going to resolve all your needs. It's going to move all your pain and it is by fall the best product, the best deal in the marketplace. That's the words that would imply certainty without the right tonality. Now let me say to you again, Jim, let me just tell you, this is the best product in the marketplace. It's going to resolve all your needs, remove all your pain and there is nothing close to it in the marketplace. Now you tell me which one of those sounds better to you. The second one, right? Jim, this is the best product. Jim, this is the best product. Now notice that when I employ certainty, I'm not yelling. Jim, this is the best. I'm not yelling over that guy on that f**king Pizelle thing. Holy little. He'd be on the infrosho. Why am I Pizelle on infrosho? That's not certainty. That's like a f**king circus attraction. And maybe it works like infrosho onto you. That's just blast shitting your ear, right? But in the world of influence and persuasion and selling, that is not the way it works. You don't yell and scream to create certainty. Certainly to be a whisper, but it's a power and it just, you say, Jim, this is so, and it's an intensity. You're speaking with staccato like beats. Boom, boom, boom. It's not passive communication. So that's your first tonality. It's a tonality of absolute certainty. Absolute certainty. Okay, not absolute. It's absolute. You will bring the voice with you. It's a volcano, a seabing volcano. I'm trying all these different ways to explain to you. Just give you one, one will hit you and you'll be able to manifest that yourself. Okay. So it's absolute certainty. That's number one. Okay. Number two, second tonality. We're going to please look at it. It's going to be sincerity or utter sincerity. Now, utter sincerity is not one certainty. You're not going to be. It's sincerity is calm. It's a velvety soft tone where you're talking from the heart and the words. So now watch the words that, when I talk about certainty, but I'm using a tonality of certainty, the words that I want my prospect to be hearing are, damn, does he believe this? Wow. He is really certain. So my, there's their hearing. I said, Jim, this is so, he's like, wow, he really needs it. That's what they're thinking as you speak. When I use the sincerity of my John, let me say this. What they say, wow, he's being really honest here. He's being honest. He's being forthright. He really is a sincere person. He means what he says. There's no pressure involved here. That's the point I'm getting across with the tonality of sincerity. So I would say, watch John, you give me one shot and believe me. So that first thought was certainty and believing the only problem you'll have, silky smooth. The only problem you'll have is that you're going to get six months ago and get you started then. That's sincerity. Silky smooth. The only problem you'll have is that you're going to get six months ago and get you started then. Silky smooth. No pressure. Heart to heart. Friend to friend. I'm speaking since these are the emotions that you're looking to get across when you use the tonality of sincerity. So if you say so in John, John, John, the only problem you'll have is you need to call me six months ago. What the fuck? They'll be like, I don't trust the person. John, the only problem you'll have is you need to call me six months ago and get started then. Now that's the third tone out, which is called your reasonable man. And the reasonable man tone is a tone out that implies that what you're saying is totally reasonable. It's like, you know, anyone would do this. You say, John, the only problem you'll have is that you get started six months ago because you'd already be making money. And now here comes the reasonable man tone. Sound fair enough? Sound good? I'm reasonable. Your reasonable. Those are the applying words that your prospect will hear when you use the reasonable man tone. You get it? In other words, they're hearing your voice. You say, John, you're giving one shot. That's certainty. And believe me, the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. That's sincerity. And then the third tone is sound fair enough to get thrown. Sound fair? I'm reasonable. You're like, you have 60 seconds. I'd like to share an idea with you. You got it? You got it? I'm reasonable. You're reasonable. So you have to say, imagine if you said, hey, John, you got 60 minutes. We're both reasonable people. I'm sure you have 60 seconds. You can't say the words, but the tonality gets your point across. It's the extra words that your prospect hears. So you'd say, so now let me watch. I'm going to link these three together, both with the rounds, to show you how you'd close the sale. And in the tonalities we used to close it. Well, I go through my whole picture and I'm asking to the owner, and I say, and John, believe me, the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. Sound fair enough? And then you shut up. Those are your first three tonalities. You use these tonalities every time you close, you're going to use those three. Use them in many other places as well. Okay? One of them will fall, please. Next, use your toes to do that one. Okay? The next tonality is the tonality of scarcity. Urgency. They go together. Scarcity is the basic human notion, basic human sense. It's how we operate. We always want more of what we perceive there to be less of. When something is in short supply or about to run out, we want to get it more, because we're scared of missing out. And this goes back to the caveman days when food was in short supply and junk was in short supply. Nowadays we refer to it as success, and he's got a scarcity mentality. She's got a scarcity mentality. They're not positive people. They're thinking from the short, as less of stuff versus more. And that's true. It comes to the overarching principles of success. But when it comes to influencing persuasion, scarcity is a very, very powerful tool. Now there are three types of scarcity. Now, break it down for you. The first one is called verbal scarcity. And verbal scarcity is simply saying words that imply scarcity. Let's say we're talking about a BMW, a car, you're selling cars. You say, oh, there's only one of these BMWs left in black on black interior. So if you don't get it now, you get six months before you order another one. Those are words that imply something is in short supply. There's only six of these lobes of bread left. We're not voting for another month after that. So that's just basic words that imply scarcity. Now let's kick it up a notch by applying the tonality of scarcity on top of the words. So you could say, oh, there's only one. This is the last one, black on black BMW 750iL. And we're not voting on the next year. Or you could say, yeah, Jim, this is the only black on black 750iL BMW. And we're not voting on the next year. Which one sounds better? Which one hits you in the gut saying, shit, if I want this. I'm just going to buy because if they were thinking about it, this is why they're at now. So we've walked into scarcity to create urgency. And so it's not enough that you get something to buy. You want to buy now. And that's the power of scarcity. So again, you have verbal scarcity, which is your words. Something is in short supply. Second, you have your tonal scarcity, which says, OK, it's in short supply. And my tonality lets you know how short that supply is. And it's running out. It's not a whisper of power. And the third type of scarcity is called informational scarcity. Meaning, even information itself is in short supply. So you have to be able to say, Jim, there's only one BMW black on black, 10iL, OK, we're not voting on the next one. And no one even knows there's only one left. Next week, we're going to actually make it an announcement. There's only one to go. How long is it going to last then? In other words, even the fact that it's in short supply, the information itself is in short supply. So those three together, they are very powerful in propelling a prospect to take action. So we want to use all three whenever possible. OK, that's your tonality of scarcity. And once the next one is over, fun. Money aside, OK, so your money aside tonality, I also call your... I'm the Fed. I'm the Fed is teaching. Now, I think one of the first topics I covered with these live traders was the process of looping. Looping is the secret source to the back half of the straight line system. Meaning, it's what we use to take the common objections that typically come up at the end of the sale, right after the big presentation, and how we take those common objections and use them as opportunities to create more certainty for each of the three pens, and then we seamlessly transition into a close without breaking the report. That's looping, right? Now, the way we begin our first loop is using this hypothetical or money aside for now, like he explained. I asked for the order from the first slide, right? So, the way we do that is we use a straight line process called deflection. We use a deflection. Deflection means we do not enter that initial objection head on. We deflect it instead and we transition to a more productive top, mainly our first ten, meaning the product. What you want to know is does the prospect, how high are they on the surface? The product, if you want to know is does the prospect, how high are they on the certainty scale in terms of our product? So, here's the words that we say. Jim, I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? I repeat, those are the words. Jim, I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Those are about as totalist as I can do. I have really good tone-ups. It's fun if you almost have no tone-ups. That's pretty talk-ready. I want you to listen to me again, and now I'm going to say it through the wrong way. So, imagine I'm just going through my presentation. I've asked you for the order from the first time, and your response was, let me think about it. And I say, well, Jim, I hear what you're saying. Let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And they'll be like, what the fuck? I'll be like, I don't know. It's alright. No, not really. You back, you get someone completely off-filtered, you break the floor, they no longer interested in talking to you or that's the one that's an aggressive tone-out. It almost an angry tone-out. You race with some certainty. Versus this tone-out. And I want you to listen very closely, and I want you to listen to your own monologue. Tell me what words you're thinking as I say this. So, I'm getting my presentation. I ask for the order, and you say, let me think about it. And I say, I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Now, the words that you probably heard are sure, or no hypotheticals, you speak money aside, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Not, well, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? What the fuck? One would go like, oh, whoa, whoa, no, sorry, click. You don't know where to go, or they'll say, yeah, or if I say, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Fair enough, but that's the idea that makes sense to you. Do you like the idea? And they'll say, because you said, so you didn't actually use the words, money aside, if you use those words, no, you made a mistake, because now you've lost control of the conversation. You've taken money out of the equation, and you made it a purely academic. That's why you still don't do that. You don't have to do that. You don't have to say those words. You don't have to say, OK, Jim, again, I know you're not going now. That's what you're saying. You're saying, no, I know this is not the real one. No, don't say that. That's the power of that tone-out. We use it in many other spots as well, but that's really important. Now let's go to number six. Next one is boom. Question versus declarative. Now, this is the very first tone-out I spoke about when I was just warming up. I was going to start telling you about the different sites and stuff. I said, how do you control someone's unconscious monologue? They're in a monologue. I said, from those very first words that you've got, hey, it's like Jordan Belfort going, hey, it's Jordan Belfort calling. Or it's, hi, it's hi, it's hi, it's hi, I'm Jordan Belfort. Hi, this is Jordan Belfort. That's a declaration. Now watch this chart here. Hi, I'm Jordan Belfort. That's flat. Almost, hi, I'm Jordan Belfort. Almost a bit of a down tone. That's a declaration, a statement I made. Hi, I'm Jordan Belfort. That's a declaration or statement. But watch this now. Watch if I flip this into the tonality of a question. I said, hey, I'm Jordan, hey, is Jordan Belfort? Question. So I up-tone, hey, hi, is Jordan Belfort calling from XYZ Company over in New York? Question, question versus hi, I'm Jordan Belfort calling XYZ Company in New York. So three down beats for declarations, up beats for questions, right? So I say, hi, I'm Jordan Belfort. I'm saying Jordan Belfort calling from XYZ Company over in New York. And they're like, now when you phrase what you've just done is you've taken a declaration and you've phrased it as a question. What that does to someone is that, they're forced now and you ask, they're saying, do I know Jordan Belfort? I don't want to be out of the loop here and be rude. So people don't want to feel out of the loop. So if you ask it, hey, Jordan, nobody out, you know who you're saying. That's what they're hearing. They're like, do I know this person? The way tonality works, in pyramid, is that when you use a certain tonality, it causes the prospect to hear additional words on top of the words that you actually said. So what you do is you put their conscious mind essentially into overdrive and try to keep up with all these additional words and besties. And it means that you layer it on top of your words. Enter their world with us. What you're probably doing right now is you're coming on a bit too strong. And Kambali has prices, price. Next. Where the deals that start via text, is there a way to translate tone to text? Listen, I take tremendous care when I write my emails to make sure that I get my tonality across in my emails by putting things in italicized, bolting them, making sure my sentences are short. It gets a little bit harder when you're texting because text by nature are supposed to be shorter. So it's a bit more challenging. But remember, you can't italicize and bolting those texts in platforms, right? So I would keep my text short if you're trying to use a text word you've provided through another platform. It's a very different way to close someone just by text. You can get them to another platform just by text and then close them there. That's the truth. All right, last question. How do I fight against gatekeepers? Well, you know, again, the basic elements of gatekeepers are that you can't get through every gatekeeper, but the general rules of gatekeepers, that number one, the less information is better.
